It breaks because of the logic that infers label positioning from the axes.  In general, we want the same label to display at the same vertical position, eg when plotting overlapping chord or note segmentations.  But when you have a multi-level segmentation, you want each level to be a distinct set of labels.  BUT you also don't want to have to append the segment level index onto the labels.

Anyway, since there are not yet standard conventions for handling multi-level segmentations, I think we're in a decent position to require that labels are disjoint across levels.

I think this should be left as `airflow.utils.decorators`. The `airflow.utils` hack has been deprecated:

```
def apply_defaults(func):
    warnings.warn_explicit(
        """
        You are importing apply_defaults from airflow.utils which
        will be deprecated in a future version.
        Please use :

        from airflow.utils.decorators import apply_defaults
        """,
        category=PendingDeprecationWarning,
        filename=func.__code__.co_filename,
        lineno=func.__code__.co_firstlineno + 1
    )
    return _apply_defaults(func)
```

So my thoughts here is that airflow operators run synchronously. They either own a thread entirely (like in the LocalExecutor, SequentialExecutor), or they completely own the machine (i.e. KubernetesExecutor, CeleryExecutor). We also want to block here because we're waiting for the pod to succeed or fail before we return the final status to the operator.

Note that the flattened spec you're creating is not a valid Swagger spec. Note that [according to the spec](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#definitionsObject), the definitions object is a key/value mapping; the mapping being Schema objects. In your case, it's a mapping of a mapping of schema objects. Consequently, adding this spec to the [Swagger editor](https://editor.swagger.io/) produces a validation error. Similarly, swagger_spec_validator complains about this fact as well.

Not really, at least not without violating import best practices (we could technically put everything in `mypy_types` and do `from mypy_types import *`, but that's rightfully considered an antipattern). We need these imports when the `mypy` static analyzer runs, but CPython 3.5.0 and 3.5.1, both of which we support, have some inconsistencies in the `typing` module. That's ok, because the `mypy` analyzer can't run in those versions anyway, but we need the code itself to run under those versions.

```python
self.unchecked_collation_added_logs = collections.defaultdict(list)

```

Then you can access both `self.unchecked_collation_added_logs[shard_id]` and `self.new_collation_added_logs[shard_id]` as if they have already been initialized thanks to the magic of defaultdicts.

This should allow you to remove all of the initialization and `ensure_` initialization code.

Still a minor difference: `os.getcwd()` expands symlinks (and `.` and `..`). In contrast, `os.environ['PWD']` does not, but of course this is not updated by `os.chdir`. Up to you, I suppose…
```
$ cd /tmp
$ ln -s /etc mylink
$ cd mylink
$ python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Oct 26 2016, 20:30:19) 
[GCC 4.8.4] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import os
>>> os.environ['PWD']
'/tmp/mylink'
>>> os.getcwd()
'/etc'
```
See https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/d4b93e21c2664d6a78e0656e7a7be0807be1c352/Modules/posixmodule.c#L3330 and `man 2 getcwd`: "The **getcwd**() function copies an *absolute pathname* of the current working directory" (emphasis mine).

It's getting messy with the macro: `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_ENABLE_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT`

What if we *REMOVED* that macro, and did this another way.

today - the NCP provides an strong label for otPlatLog() function.
today - the CLI provides an strong label for otPlatLog() function.

What if both of these implementations of otPlatLog() (CLI & NCP) go away.

We replace them with (below) - they would write to the same place the old function writes to, (the CLI writes to the CLI console server, the NCP writes to the SPINEL channel - but takes an "va_list" parameter instead.

```
void otPlatCliLogV( otLogLevel aLogLevel, otLogRegion aLogRegion, const char *aFormat, va_list ap)
void otPlatNcpLogV( otLogLevel aLogLevel, otLogRegion aLogRegion, const char *aFormat, va_list ap)

```
Then, the  "main.c" implementation for  CLI and NCP.

The application then chooses if it wants:

a)  No log output

b) Log output using the existing channel (ie: CLI console server, or NCP channel)
(in this case the log function just calls the CliLogV() or NcpLogV() function accordingly)

c) To use the DEBUG uart - which calls the DebugLogV() function.

This is sort of a tautology, I guess. We can say a stack is "valid" if it has an empty `InFlightOperations`, and then yeah, having a non-empty `InFlightOperations` would render the stack invalid.

In general, it will be possible for stacks outside of an update to have non-empty `InFlightOperations`. I'm not sure we want to commit to call this stack "invalid". The engine will reject it and it'll ask you to do `pulumi stack export | pulumi stack import` to fix it, but there's nothing fundamentally wrong about the stack that would e.g. prevent it from being inspected in meaningful ways by the service.
